summary: Housing development is booming along the SE BeltLine, with new projects breaking ground in Reynoldstown and Decatur finally moving forward. Meanwhile, TSA chaos at the airport has ICE agents patrolling terminals, and parents across DeKalb are fired up about proposed school closures.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown BeltLine Corner Development Finally Gets Moving
topStory.summary: After sitting empty for nearly five years, that cleared lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. Atlanta-based Embry Development is breaking ground on 85 townhomes and condos just a block from the BeltLine's Eastside Trail. The project switched hands from Toll Brothers to Embry two years ago, and now it looks like they're actually making it happen.
topStory.body:
You know that corner lot at Chester and Fulton Terrace that's been sitting there cleared and empty since 2021? Well, finally something's happening. Atlanta-based Embry Development Company has started groundwork on what will become 85 residential units about a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail.

The project, now called Camber Crossing, had been stuck in development limbo after Toll Brothers originally permitted it. Embry bought the 2-acre site two years ago for the project located at 195 Chester Ave., and according to their managing partner, they're looking at a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos. It's exactly the kind of development Reynoldstown has been seeing more of as the BeltLine continues to drive demand for walkable housing.

This is just one more sign that the southeast BeltLine corridor is still hot, even as the broader housing market has cooled. With Memorial Drive right there and easy access to the trail, it's prime real estate for people who want to live ITP without paying Virginia-Highland prices.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site showing early groundwork at Reynoldstown BeltLine development

stories.headline: Decatur Gets Village-Style 'Missing Middle' Housing That Won't Break Bank
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is launching next week as a pocket neighborhood of 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards. The project at 2883 Midway Road aims to create more accessible ITP housing near downtown Decatur, where median home values have climbed over $700k. It's exactly the kind of missing middle housing Atlanta needs more of.
stories.body:
Remember when you could buy a house near Decatur without selling a kidney? Those days feel long gone with median home values topping $700,000, but Cottages at Midway is trying to bring some sanity back to the market. The project is launching its soft opening next week for 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards and greenspace.

Located at 2883 and 2893 Midway Road, the pocket neighborhood is replacing two underused lots totaling 2.1 acres on a residential street. The goal is creating more accessible, single-family ITP housing near a major commercial corridor and downtown Decatur. It's that sweet spot between a cramped condo and a million-dollar Craftsman.

This is exactly the kind of missing middle housing Atlanta desperately needs more of. Dense enough to make financial sense, but still giving you your own front door and some outdoor space. With construction already underway, it looks like they might actually deliver on making Decatur living slightly more affordable for regular humans.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for Cottages at Midway showing house layout and shared spaces

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: Those West End townhomes near Lee + White are finally rising up along the BeltLine. GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) will bring 23 for-sale units to 600 Hopkins Street, with the first seven properties hitting the market this spring. It's rare to see new for-sale townhomes along this stretch of the southwest BeltLine.
stories.body:
If you've been walking the BeltLine's southwest stretch near Lee + White, you've probably noticed the construction at 600 Hopkins Street finally looking like actual buildings. GW Parkside, developed by the same folks who own Lee + White, is bringing 23 new townhomes to the area with the first seven units nearing completion.

Formerly called Hopkins and White Townhomes, the project started with demolition a year ago and is now showing real progress. The initial seven properties are hitting the market this spring, according to onsite signage. It's actually pretty rare to see new for-sale townhomes along this particular stretch of the southwest BeltLine, where most new development has been apartments.

Ackerman & Co. is betting there's demand for homeownership options along the trail, especially in an area that's seen so much investment with Lee + White's expansion of office, retail, and food offerings. With the BeltLine right there and downtown just a short ride away, it could be a smart play for people who want to own something walkable without paying eastside prices.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of West End townhomes near BeltLine

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, Peking chicken platters for two, and cocktails like Thai tea martinis. It's the kind of Asian-American comfort food this neighborhood has been missing.
stories.body:
After years of working in other people's kitchens (including Gordon Ramsay's empire in NYC), chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez finally have their own place. Mushi Ni opened yesterday on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, just a quick walk from the Freedom Park Trail.

The menu hits that sweet spot of Asian-American comfort food with dishes like soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, and umami fries. Their Peking chicken platter feeds two people for $25, which feels like a steal these days. They're also doing cocktails, including a Thai tea martini that sounds dangerous in the best way possible.

Both chefs have serious credentials (Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay, Le has deep Atlanta restaurant experience), and their pop-up events around town built up quite the following. Having a permanent home in Inman Park puts them right in the heart of the BeltLine crowd, and honestly, this part of town could use more interesting food options beyond the usual suspects.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style BBQ to Ansley Mall
stories.summary: The smokehouse at Lewis Barbecue might be the fanciest in Georgia, with six 22-foot smokers running 24/7 in a loft space with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. John Lewis, who helped create the smokers from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, is turning out some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta.
stories.body:
Most barbecue joints hide their smokers in back alleys or windowless sheds, but Lewis Barbecue put theirs in a glass-walled loft that looks like the world's meatiest art installation. Six 22-foot smokers (welded from 1,000-gallon propane tanks by pitmaster John Lewis himself) run around the clock in eight-hour shifts, visible to anyone walking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine.

The setup at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse is unlike anything I've seen in Atlanta. Lewis brought his Austin expertise (by way of Charleston) to create what's essentially a brisket factory with panoramic windows. The smokers run 24 hours a day in a staggered rhythm that produces some seriously good barbecue.

What makes this special isn't just the Instagram-worthy smokehouse setup. Lewis actually knows what he's doing, and the brisket shows it. In a city where good barbecue can be hit or miss, having someone with this level of technique and commitment to the craft is a real win for the BeltLine corridor.
stories.imageAlt: BBQ platter at Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Food Hall Opens in East Lake This April
stories.summary: The guy who created NYC's Gansevoort Market is opening his third Atlanta food hall at the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works will feature six chef-driven concepts with craft cocktails and outdoor dining, set to open mid-April. It's the kind of food destination the eastside has been waiting for.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid knows how to create a food destination. The founder of NYC's legendary Gansevoort Market has already done two successful food halls in Atlanta, and now he's bringing his magic to the eastside with Vela Park Food Works.

The new food hall is the centerpiece of the Vela Park rental community near East Lake Golf Club, featuring six different chef-driven concepts alongside craft cocktails and roomy outdoor dining. Construction is wrapping up now, with a mid-April opening date set. Given Montwaid's track record, this should be something special.

What I love about this is the location. The eastside has been underserved when it comes to quality food destinations, especially compared to the westside or BeltLine corridor. Having a proper food hall with multiple concepts gives people options beyond the usual chain restaurants, and the outdoor space should be perfect for Atlanta's long dining season.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing dining areas

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Project Finally Taking Shape
stories.summary: West Howard Avenue's transformation from a high-speed drag strip to a people-friendly Complete Street is almost done. The project includes a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA. It's been nearly a decade in the making, but it looks like it was worth the wait.
stories.body:
If you've driven West Howard Avenue lately, you've probably noticed it looks completely different. The roughly one-mile stretch between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA station is getting the full Complete Streets treatment, and it's almost finished after starting construction in August.

The project includes a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, upgraded sidewalks, and traffic calming infrastructure from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School to the city's western limits at Paden Circle. For most of the route, there's a concrete-protected bike lane that actually makes cycling feel safe rather than suicidal.

This has been nearly a decade in the making, and honestly, it was desperately needed. West Howard used to be a four-lane speedway where cars treated pedestrians and cyclists as inconveniences. The new design prioritizes people over cars, which is exactly what a street connecting downtown to MARTA should do. It's the kind of infrastructure that makes living without a car actually viable in the suburbs.
stories.imageAlt: Protected bike lane on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: ICE Agents Patrol Atlanta Airport as TSA Chaos Continues
stories.summary: Armed federal agents in tactical gear are now walking the terminals at Hartsfield-Jackson as the government shutdown drives massive delays. TSA agents are working without pay, and yesterday 41% called out sick in Atlanta. The agents aren't doing immigration enforcement but are there for security support as four-hour wait times become the norm.
stories.body:
Walking through Hartsfield-Jackson yesterday felt like being in a different airport. Armed ICE agents in tactical gear were patrolling the terminals, not for immigration enforcement but as backup security while TSA agents work without pay and call out sick in record numbers.

The numbers are staggering. Yesterday, 41% of TSA agents in Atlanta called out, according to DHS. Four-hour security wait times have become routine, and the airport is telling travelers to arrive that early just to make their flights. The ICE deployment is supposed to provide additional security presence, though they're not actually screening passengers or bags.

This is what happens when you don't fund critical infrastructure. TSA agents are approaching their second missed paycheck, and frankly, who can blame them for not showing up to work they're not getting paid for? The ripple effects are hitting everyone who flies through the world's busiest airport, which is pretty much everyone in the Southeast at some point.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Atlanta Council Advances ICE Restrictions Despite Fire Union Drama
stories.summary: The City Council's Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions barring ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and requiring police to document ICE activities during joint operations. But the bigger drama was dozens of residents criticizing Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council took a strong stance against ICE operations yesterday, unanimously advancing two resolutions through the Public Safety Committee. One bars Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facilities within city limits, while another requires Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations. Both go to the full council for an April 20 vote.

But the real fireworks came from dozens of residents blasting Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' union contract. Council already ratified Atlanta's first-ever firefighter union agreement back in April 2025, but Dickens won't put pen to paper. The International Association of Fire Fighters says this is the first city council meeting since the contract stalemate began.

The ICE resolutions are largely symbolic (federal agents will do what they want regardless), but they send a clear message about Atlanta's values. The firefighter contract dispute, though, has real consequences for people who risk their lives keeping us safe. It's hard to understand why the mayor won't sign something council already approved.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with residents speaking

stories.headline: DeKalb Parents Rage Against School Closure Plans
stories.summary: Hundreds of frustrated parents packed Lakeside High on March 23 for another DeKalb Schools input session on potential closures. Parents from Oak Grove and Evansdale elementary schools are calling the process a farce, saying district officials aren't really listening to community concerns about the Student Assignment Project.
stories.body:
The scene outside Lakeside High was telling. Parents and kids from Oak Grove and Evansdale elementary schools held signs advocating for small schools and a people-first approach while hundreds more packed inside for what many called another exercise in frustration with DeKalb County Schools.

Samuel Howe, an Oak Grove parent, didn't mince words: 'They are giving us lip service, they're saying they're listening to us, it's a process. This is not a process. This is a farce, and they're not listening.' The district is holding these public input sessions on their revised list of potential school closures and building changes, but parents feel like decisions have already been made.

DeKalb's Student Assignment Project has become a lightning rod for community anger about transparency and process. When you're talking about closing neighborhood schools, you better believe parents are going to fight tooth and nail. The district says they need to right-size their facilities, but parents see their kids' schools and communities being destroyed for budget reasons.
stories.imageAlt: Parents and children protesting outside Lakeside High School

stories.headline: Seven-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in DeKalb
stories.summary: A 7-year-old boy died overnight in what police are calling an accidental discharge of a weapon at a home on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood. Family members say the child got hold of a gun and shot himself. It's a devastating reminder of how quickly unsecured firearms can turn tragic in homes with children.
stories.body:
DeKalb County police responded to a home on Loveless Drive in the Ellenwood area during the early morning hours and found a 7-year-old boy dead inside. Family members at the scene told reporters the boy was playing with a firearm when it discharged, and investigators off-camera confirmed they're treating it as a 'non-intended discharge of a weapon.'

The investigation drew a large crowd of grieving family and friends to the neighborhood, with the roadway blocked off for several hours during the initial investigation. While official details remain limited, this appears to be another tragic case of an unsecured weapon in a home with children.

These accidents are preventable, and that's what makes them so heartbreaking. Gun safes, trigger locks, keeping ammunition separate from firearms - there are ways to have guns in your home while keeping kids safe. But it only takes one moment of a weapon not being properly secured for a family to lose everything. My heart goes out to this family dealing with an unimaginable loss.
stories.imageAlt: DeKalb County police investigating at residence

stories.headline: Man Killed Outside Vine City Market
stories.summary: A man died after being shot Tuesday night outside a market on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard in Vine City. Atlanta police found him shot just before 9 p.m. and rushed him to the hospital, where he died. Police haven't released details about what led to the shooting or any suspect information.
stories.body:
Another deadly shooting hit northwest Atlanta Tuesday night when a man was gunned down outside a Vine City market. It happened just before 9 p.m. in the 900 block of Joseph E. Boone Boulevard NW, just east of Joseph E. Lowery Boulevard.

Atlanta police found the victim with gunshot wounds and rushed him to an area hospital, but he didn't survive. Police haven't released the victim's identity or any information about suspects, and it's still unclear what led to the shooting or if any arrests have been made.

This is part of a troubling pattern of gun violence that keeps hitting Atlanta's neighborhoods. Just about a mile away on Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard, there was another shooting the same night outside a barber shop. While police haven't connected the two incidents, it shows how gun violence ripples through communities and leaves families shattered.
stories.imageAlt: Police scene at Vine City shooting location

stories.headline: Eight-Foot Python Shows Up on the BeltLine
stories.summary: Someone spotted and filmed an 8- to 10-foot python on the BeltLine, with video showing a man handling the snake after pulling it from bushes. Wildlife officials are warning people not to handle unknown animals they find, and it's unclear if this was someone's escaped pet or something more concerning for the trail's ecosystem.
stories.body:
Just when you thought the BeltLine couldn't get any weirder, someone captured video of a massive python being yanked out of the bushes by what appears to be a random passerby. Joseph Ragland filmed the incident and shared it with Channel 2, saying he was trying to see the snake's head when 'somebody's uncle comes out of nowhere and starts yanking it out of the bush.'

The snake was 8 to 10 feet long, definitely not native to Georgia, and definitely not something you should handle if you're not a professional. Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue called the video 'very concerning,' pointing out that grabbing an unknown snake is dangerous both for the person and potentially for the animal.

Nobody knows if this was someone's escaped pet or how it ended up on the trail, but it's a reminder that the BeltLine attracts all kinds of wildlife (intentional and otherwise). Wildlife officials say if you see something like this, call professionals rather than trying to handle it yourself. Leave the snake wrangling to people who know what they're doing.
